title: From Chicago to Boeing
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Seattle on February 28, 2013, Elmer Dixon traces his parents from Chicago, a Kentucky-born father raised there and a mother whose people came up from Mississippi in the great migration, and the illustrator job at Boeing that brought the family to the Northwest in 1957.

title: From Revolutionary to Interculturalist
summary: Dixon describes his decades of diversity and cross-cultural work, how his Black Panther past stayed hidden from corporate clients, and the 2010 SIETAR Europa congress in Lyon, France, where the theme of human rights and cultural diversity let him give a talk titled From Revolutionary to Interculturalist.

title: Landing in the Melting Pot
summary: He contrasts the segregated South Side of Chicago with the Madrona home his mother still occupies, moved into in 1960, a mixed neighborhood of Black, Chinese, Japanese, Filipino, and Latino families where the playground across the street was the gathering place and a redlining boundary ran two blocks away.

title: Greeted by Boos, Not Confetti
summary: Dixon recounts his father and friends forming a group called the Four Feathers and volunteering for the draft after Pearl Harbor, only to be sent to a segregated camp in Mississippi, where the town that should have cheered departing soldiers instead booed, spat, and hurled slurs at the Black men preparing to defend America.

title: No One Was Going to Attack His Kid
summary: He explains that such incidents never soured his parents on other people, who taught their children to judge by the content of character, and tells of throwing eggs at passing cars as a boy, getting grabbed by a driver, and his father seizing a hammer and charging out the door to defend his son.

title: Wax the Floor Days
summary: Dixon remembers a house full of music, Ellington and Count Basie for Black guests and opera like La Boheme played while the boys waxed the hardwood floors for the Scandinavian friends who came to dance, plus matzo balls at a Jewish family's Passover, Russian Orthodox Christmas foods, and the Chinese shopkeeper who called him bad boy for years after a stolen candy bar.

title: Outgoing and Adventurous
topic: What He Took From His Father
summary: He reflects on the traits he absorbed from his charismatic, easy-to-make-friends father at Boeing, who had progressive friends and possibly contacts in civil rights circles, and the spirit of adventure, fed by the TV show Adventures in Paradise and the Kon-Tiki, that became his own can-do attitude.

title: A Series of Micro-Abrasions
summary: Dixon describes the family's dinner-table routine and watching the nightly news, the assassination of Medgar Evers and a church bombing, Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge with demonstrators beaten and hosed, and a colleague's metaphor of racism as a series of micro-abrasions, a wound repeatedly picked at that never heals.

title: Go Hear This Speaker at Garfield
summary: He recalls the Black awareness movement reaching Seattle around 1965 while he still skateboarded with his white friends down at Crater Lake, his brother Aaron awakening before him, and Aaron telling him in 1967 that he needed to go hear a speaker named Stokely Carmichael at Garfield High School.

title: The Good Knight on the White Horse
summary: Dixon relays Carmichael's argument that Black people had been programmed to hate themselves, illustrated by the good knight always on a white horse and the bad on a black, white-hatted cowboys against Dirty Bart, angel food cake against devil's food, claims the teenager first dismissed but began turning over while sitting on the tennis courts across from his home.

title: They Thought We Were the Help
summary: He recounts his Madrona tennis team, coached by a volunteer named Miss Sullivan, winning the right to play the city championship at the all-white Seattle Tennis Club across the red line, where a Black kitchen worker mistook the players for hired help, the club barred Black members, and officials left before presenting the trophies the team had won, alongside his mother being told a job was filled until she called back unrecognized.

title: Co-Chair of Seattle SNCC
summary: Dixon describes knowing he and his brother had to get involved, Aaron entering the Black Student Union at the University of Washington alongside Larry Gossett, and himself, a high school senior, joining SNCC as co-chair of the Seattle chapter, where the organizing centered on voter registration.

title: Bing Bing Bang Bang, Black Power
summary: He tells how he and a few friends pushed for a Black Student Union at Garfield over the principal's refusal, agitated by sitting through the Pledge and the anthem and leading a Black Power chant that erupted the whole student body, then called a boycott that filled the halls until the principal relented, making it the first high school Black Student Union on the West Coast.

title: Charged With Inciting a Riot
summary: Dixon recounts landing in the middle of a disturbance outside rival Franklin High School, objecting as two officers pinned a Black girl named Deborah Rodriguez to the ground, being handcuffed and charged with inciting a riot, and a judge who, rather than jailing him, asked him to write an essay on why he defended her.

title: We Took Over the Principal's Office
summary: He describes the grievances at Franklin, two Black students expelled over a fight while white students were sent back to class and a girl expelled for wearing an afro, and how he, Larry Gossett, his brother Aaron, and others occupied the principal's office overnight, answering the phones until the administration agreed to address the demands and grant a BSU.

title: Arrested the Day King Was Killed
summary: Dixon tells of being pulled from his geometry class about a week later, read his rights and arrested for unlawful assembly, while Aaron, Larry Gossett, and other leaders were arrested almost simultaneously and, within the hour, Martin Luther King was assassinated, leaving him in juvenile watching cities burn and his parents driving him home in silence with both sons jailed.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: They Knew Something Was Happening
summary: He reflects that his cross-cultural upbringing was mostly unspoken and that he never sat down to discuss his politicization with his parents, who nonetheless saw the signs, a newspaper photo of the seventeen-year-old in an afro and SNCC jacket telling reporters why he thought Vietnam, unlike his father's patriotic World War II service, was not a war worth fighting.

title: Like Landing in a Black Army
summary: Dixon recounts traveling to the West Coast Black Student Union conference at San Francisco State in spring 1968, learning that the young Panther Bobby Hutton had been shot by Oakland police the day before King died, and crossing into West Oakland for the funeral, where Panthers in leather jackets and berets stood in platoon formation like a Black army.

title: That Could Be Me Lying There
summary: He describes the overwhelming pride and defiance of seeing an organized force so unlike the singing demonstrators on TV, spotting Marlon Brando talking with Bobby Seale, and stepping into a church lined with armed young Panthers where Bobby Hutton lay in an open casket draped in a Panther flag, seventeen years old like Dixon, the moment he decided there was no turning back and felt no fear of giving his life.

title: Fifty Panthers in My Parents' Home
summary: Dixon recalls Bobby Seale's fiery speech that night at the conference, cornering him with Aaron and a few others to ask to start a Seattle chapter, and Seale arriving two weeks later to about fifty would-be Panthers spread across his parents' living room floor, organizing the chapter with Aaron as defense captain and Dixon as field lieutenant.

part: What It Meant to Be a Panther
title: Dead Before Twenty-Five
summary: He explains the lifelong commitment Seale laid out, how most serious Panthers, himself seventeen and Aaron nineteen, expected to be dead before twenty-five and those unwilling to accept that left within months, and how in 1968 J. Edgar Hoover named the Black Panther Party the number one threat to the internal security of the United States, painting a bull's-eye on them.

title: A Defensive Animal
summary: Dixon describes the party organizing not around civil rights but around God-given rights to humanity and dignity, taking the panther symbol from the Lowndes County Freedom Organization because the black panther is a defensive animal, becoming the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense, whose first survival program was the police alert patrol against routine racist brutality.

title: Armed With the Law
topic: The Oakland Standoff and Huey's Mystique
summary: He recounts how Huey Newton and Bobby Seale armed themselves with the Constitution and the California Penal Code and studied Mao's Red Book, then faced down Oakland police with shotguns on a street corner, Huey jacking a round into his chamber and declaring his right to the weapon, a moment that birthed the Panther mystique while leaving communities mistaking the party for their personal defense force.

title: Two Guns and a Thousand Rounds
summary: Dixon summarizes the Ten-Point Program, demands for an end to police brutality, true education, healthcare, food, land, housing, justice, exemption from military service, and juries of one's peers, and the discipline it required, every Panther owning two guns and a thousand rounds and reading two hours a day to become a full revolutionary.

title: It Exploded Overnight
topic: From a Flood to a Hard Core
summary: He describes how Bobby Hutton's death drove a flood of recruits, four to five hundred Panthers in Seattle within weeks drawn by pride and belonging, most used to spontaneous rebellion rather than the patient work of building political power, and the political education classes and readings like The Wretched of the Earth that the movement demanded.

title: Boiled Down to Twenty
summary: Dixon recalls the chapter shrinking within months to a hardcore group of about twenty, the funerals of Seattle Panthers that drew thousands, the macho pride of the uniform balanced against the discipline required, and the infighting and expulsions that he saw as a natural part of forging a close-knit, family-like organization out of people putting their lives on the line.

title: A Bodyguard on the Porch
topic: Threats and the First Breakfast Program
summary: He tells of moving into his own apartment after graduating Garfield in June 1968 with two bodyguards at seventeen, a bodyguard sleeping on his parents' porch, midnight death-threat calls his mother answered with go ahead and try, the FBI visiting his father at Boeing, and a white pastor opening his church for the chapter's first free breakfast program when Black churches had said no.

title: Prevent the Rise of a Black Messiah
summary: Dixon recounts J. Edgar Hoover's memo to destroy, disrupt, and discredit the party and prevent the rise of a Black messiah, naming Los Angeles, Chicago, and Seattle as offices to shut down, with Huey already jailed, Bunchy Carter organizing thousands of former Slausons gang members in Los Angeles, and Fred Hampton uniting Chicago's gangs and young whites and Latinos.

title: All Power to the People
summary: He describes assassination attempts on himself and Aaron, including a shotgun whose doctored ammunition exploded and nearly took Aaron's arm, and Fred Hampton coining the phrase rainbow coalition before Jesse Jackson, the party's revolutionary intercommunalism and international branches, and Seattle's coalitions with the Peace and Freedom Party, the Young Lords, the Red Guard, and the American Indian Movement.

title: Five Breakfast Programs, Two Thousand Kids
summary: Dixon ties his multicultural upbringing back to the party's intercultural character and describes five breakfast programs feeding over two thousand children a hot meal, sited in housing projects like Yesler Terrace and Holly Park and at the Atlantic Street Center, run by Ike Ikeda who had known the Dixon boys as children, sustained by a coalition of supporters including wealthy white liberals.

title: Sandbags and Gun Bunkers
summary: He recounts Dr. John Green, a University of Washington neurologist who ran a medical detail at anti-war marches, helping the party open a free medical clinic in their second headquarters at 20th and Spruce, a fortified building with the People's Wall mural, sandbags on the walls, gun bunkers, and steel-and-plywood window covers, moved there in late 1969 as offices were attacked nationwide.

title: The Clinic Still Open Today
summary: Dixon describes the clinic's first focus, a well-baby clinic addressing high Black infant mortality, mothers walking past the armed officer of the day and sandbags yet feeling secure, the clinic surviving as the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center named for a young woman who died before thirty, and a roster of over thirty programs including free busing to prisons, free food, and a summer liberation school.

title: A Subpoena to Washington
summary: He recounts the Seattle chapter being transferred to Oakland in 1971 while he served fourteen months as a political prisoner in Salem, Oregon over an allegedly stolen coat after speaking at the University of Oregon, his family arranging a clemency hearing with Governor Tom McCall, and a House Un-American Activities Committee subpoena where, after giving only his name, residence, and occupation, he took the Fifth on whether he belonged to the Black Panther Party.

title: Only an Unconditional Surrender
summary: Dixon describes the prison captain warning him and another Panther after the Attica uprising and the murder of George Jackson that they would be shot first if anything broke out, telling the governor's investigator he would accept nothing from the governor but an unconditional surrender and refusing release on those terms, and keeping up low-key organizing and political education behind bars, unlike the volatile California prisons.

title: The Sandbags Were Gone
topic: A Changed Era and the Split
summary: Paroled in late 1972, he finds the old sandbagged office replaced and a quieter era, no more guns under pillows, and addresses the famous Panther split between continuing armed militancy and focusing on survival programs, recalling Seattle's own early ideological rift around 1969, including a brother-in-law inclined toward sensationalism and others who went to prison for a bank robbery, that the chapter weathered to keep building the structure Huey and Bobby intended.

title: Perpetuated by COINTELPRO
summary: Dixon argues the national ideological split was driven by FBI informants through COINTELPRO, defines the counterintelligence program and its long history of union busting in the 1940s, McCarthy-era blacklisting, and the wiretapping of Martin Luther King, and explains how it turned its energies against the Panthers, whom Hoover had declared a greater threat even than King.
